w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: "Every day in India, millions of people take paracetamol in the morning and drink

at night. They take blood pressure medicine and also consume Arjuna bark. They take metformin for diabetes and also drink karela juice. They think they're being healthy — but some of these combinations can cause internal bleeding, liver damage, or dangerously low blood sugar. And nobody warns them."

"India is the only major country where two complete medical systems — Allopathy and AYUSH — run in parallel. Over 70% of Indians use some form of traditional remedy alongside modern medicine. But there is no safety layer between these two worlds. Doctors don't ask what herbs you're taking. Ayurvedic practitioners don't know what pills you're on. The result? Silent, preventable health emergencies that never get traced back to the real cause — unsafe drug-herb combinations."
WHY THIS IS EARLY DISEASE PREDICTION (10 seconds — this connects you to

"We're not predicting diabetes from a questionnaire — there are already apps for that. We're predicting the health emergencies that nobody else is even looking for. Drug-herb interactions cause real diseases —
, organ toxicity, metabolic crises — and we catch them before they happen."
"We built [YOUR APP NAME] — an AI-powered drug-herb interaction safety system designed for India.

A user simply selects the medicines they take and the traditional remedies or herbs they use. Our AI checks for dangerous interactions using a knowledge base built from pharmacological research and AYUSH literature. Within seconds, it shows:
The risk level — safe, moderate, or dangerous
A clear explanation of WHY the combination is risky
What action to take — stop, reduce, or consult a doctor"
THE AI (15 seconds — address the AI/ML criteria directly)

"Under the hood, we use a knowledge graph that maps drug mechanisms to herb mechanisms — for example, both aspirin and Guggul affect platelet aggregation. We use RAG — Retrieval Augmented Generation — so our AI doesn't just flag risks, it explains them in simple language by retrieving from verified medical sources. This isn't a black box. Every warning is explainable and traceable."
. Combined use may increase bleeding risk."
Action: "Consult your doctor before continuing this combination."

"This system can be deployed as a mobile app for citizens, integrated into pharmacy billing systems, or used by AYUSH and allopathic doctors during consultations. It scales to any language, any region. India's Digital Health Mission and ABHA ecosystem make this integration practical, not theoretical."
CLOSING (10 seconds — the line they remember)
"The world is building AI to replace doctors. We built AI to make sure two medical systems that serve 1.4 billion people can finally talk to each other — safely."
JUDGE Q&A PREP — have these answers ready:
"Where does your interaction data come from?"
database, AYUSH pharmacopoeia, and peer-reviewed herb-drug interaction research. Every interaction in our system is sourced."

→ "This is a screening tool, not a diagnostic device. We flag potential risks for user awareness and doctor consultation. We prioritize safety — meaning we'd rather over-flag than miss a dangerous combination."

→ "Global companies build for single-medicine systems. India's pluralistic medicine reality — where the same person uses Ayurveda, homeopathy, and allopathy — is unique. This requires Indian-context AI, not Western health tech."
"How is this AI and not just a lookup table?"
→ "The knowledge graph maps mechanisms, not just pairs. So even if a specific herb-drug pair isn't in our database, the system can infer risk based on shared pharmacological
pathways. The RAG layer generates human-readable explanations dynamically. A lookup table can't do either."
→ "API for pharmacies, integration with e-prescription platforms like Eka Care or
, and a consumer app. The Indian government's push to integrate AYUSH into the national health stack makes this timely."
Age, weight, height, existing conditions (diabetes, BP, etc.)
This is NOT a separate product
Example: If someone is 65+ and on blood thinners, the interaction risk is MORE severe than for a 25-year-old
Personalized risk based on Feature 1 (age, conditions matter)
This is what makes you unique
Feature 3: "Safe Remedy Finder" (your new idea, but SAFE version)
Instead of: "Tell me your symptoms and I'll diagnose you" (dangerous)
Do this: User says "I have a headache"
System shows: "Here are common remedies people use"
Always ends with: "Consult a doctor for persistent symptoms"
See what happened? The symptom part becomes a GATEWAY to your core interaction checker, not a separate diagnostic tool.

"A user feels stomach pain. They Google 'home remedy for stomach pain' and find ajwain or jeera water. Sounds harmless. But they're also taking antacids and blood pressure medication. Nobody tells them that some traditional remedies interact with their medicines. Our system does."
That's ONE coherent product, not three separate features competing for attention.
What to BUILD (priority order in 48 hours):
The rule: If Feature 1 isn't rock solid, don't touch Feature 3.
Your product name could be something like
Does this make sense? The key insight is: every feature you add should make the interaction checker MORE useful, not compete with it.
Node.js + Express (free) or go fully frontend-only (no server needed)
Use one of these free options:
— run models locally on your laptop (completely free, no API key)
free tier running Llama 3 is more than enough for a hackathon demo.
Just a JSON file embedded in your app (no database server needed)
This is the critical part. I found several real, usable sources:

— A published herb-drug interaction database covering 58 plants and 114 drugs, with pharmacokinetic interaction strength grades and scientific references. Built from Medline, Scopus, and Google Scholar data.

— A developer built a free, open-source herb-drug interaction checker with 590+ documented combinations covering 47 herbs and 23 drug classes. Every entry includes severity level, mechanism, and PubMed DOI. The data structure is exactly what you need — it's a JSON object you can directly use or adapt.
(drugbank.ca) — Free academic license available. Contains drug mechanisms, pathways, and interaction data.
India's Ministry of AYUSH has digital initiatives including the Traditional Knowledge Digital Library (TKDL) and AYUSH research portals
Published literature on Ayurvedic drug interactions documents concepts like
5. A 2025 paper directly validates your idea
et al. published "AI-driven prediction of Drug-Herb Interactions: A critical need in India's pluralistic health culture" arguing that AI is the right approach for this exact problem in India. This is
for your pitch — you can cite an actual academic paper that says your project is needed.
Download/adapt the Botanica Andina JSON dataset (590+ interactions)
Add 20-30 India-specific herb entries: Ashwagandha, Guggul, Tulsi, Neem, Brahmi, Arjuna, Karela,
For each Indian herb, search PubMed for known interactions (you can do this manually for 20 herbs)
"mechanism": "Both inhibit platelet aggregation, additive bleeding risk",
Build the matching logic (herb + drug → check interactions)
Add severity classification (High / Moderate / Low / Safe)
Add explanation generator using free LLM (
Send the interaction data to the LLM
LLM returns a simple, human-readable explanation
This is your RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) — retrieve from your database, generate explanation with AI
Simple form: age, weight, existing conditions (diabetes, BP, heart disease)
If user is 65+ → increase severity for bleeding-related interactions
If user has liver disease → flag herbs that affect CYP450 enzymes more strongly
If user is diabetic → flag herbs that lower blood sugar (karela + metformin =
Clean UI with dropdowns for medicines and herbs
Color-coded results (Red = dangerous, Yellow = caution, Green = safe)
"Safe Remedy Finder": user selects their current medicines → system shows which home remedies are SAFE to use
This is the reverse of the interaction checker and is genuinely useful
Add the LLM-powered explanation for each interaction
Practice the pitch I gave you earlier
Record a backup demo video (in case live demo fails)
HOW TO USE THE FREE LLM (Step by Step)
With your interaction data as context + user query
"You are a drug-herb interaction safety assistant for India.
Given this interaction data: [your JSON entry],
explain in simple language why this combination is risky
and what the user should do. Keep it under 3 sentences."
That's it. That's your "AI" — and it's real, legitimate AI use.
WHAT MAKES THIS TECHNICALLY STRONG FOR JUDGES
: Your herb-drug data structured as entities + relationships
: Retrieve from your curated database, generate explanations with LLM
for herb-drug interaction prediction achieved 95% accuracy, showing this is a legitimate and active AI research area. You don't need to build
— but mentioning this in your presentation shows you understand the space.
This is an initial search, so there may be additional free databases I didn't surface — but what's here is more than enough to build a strong prototype.
I'll be completely honest with you. Here's what my search found:
Does a drug-herb interaction app exist? Yes, partially. But NOT in the way you're building it.
— a free static HTML tool with 590+ interactions, but it's built for Western herbs (St. John's Wort, Ginkgo, Echinacea) and is in Spanish. Not India-focused at all.
— they cover drug-drug interactions well, but herb coverage is limited to a handful of Western supplements
Some AI drug interaction tools exist in concept, but they focus on drug-drug, not herb-drug, and they're enterprise products for hospitals
— an AI-powered Ayurvedic wellness platform that does Prakriti analysis and remedy recommendations. But it does NOT check interactions between Ayurvedic and allopathic medicines.
India's AYUSH ministry has digital portals (AYUSH Suraksha, TKDL) but these are reporting/research tools, not consumer-facing interaction checkers
Multiple news articles from Business Standard and India Today in 2025 discuss the PROBLEM of mixing Ayurveda and allopathy
— doctors warning about Guggul + blood thinners, Ashwagandha + sedatives,
because NO APP EXISTS to check this automatically
No consumer app in India that lets you input your allopathic medicines + Ayurvedic herbs and get instant safety alerts
No tool that covers Indian-specific herbs (Guggul, Ashwagandha, Tulsi,
No tool that personalizes risk based on your health profile (age, conditions)
A 2025 academic paper literally calls this "a critical need in India's pluralistic health culture" and recommends AI as the solution
writing papers about how someone should build exactly what you're building
, but nobody has built it yet.
of herb-drug interaction checking exists. A few Western tools do it partially. But an

that checks interactions between common Indian herbs and allopathic medicines? That does not exist as a product. The gap is real, the need is documented, and doctors in India are publicly saying this is a problem with no solution.
This is a genuinely strong idea for your data backbone. Let me break down what each database gives you and whether it's actually usable:
largest curated database of Indian medicinal plants
, hosted by the Institute of Mathematical Sciences, Chennai. It's freely accessible at cb.imsc.res.in/
Plant-part level associations (which part of the plant contains which chemical)
ADMET properties (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity) already computed
(Nature), cited nearly 600 times. The 2.0 version from 2022 doubled the coverage.
PubChem (pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) is NIH's free chemical database with:
Chemical structures for virtually all known drugs
— you can programmatically query any drug compound
CYP450 enzyme interaction data (this is KEY for predicting herb-drug interactions)
Completely free, no registration needed for basic API access.
— Useful for context, but limited for direct data.

The Ministry of AYUSH has several digital initiatives including TKDL (Traditional Knowledge Digital Library), AYUSH Suraksha Portal, e-Charak, and the Ayush Grid platform. However, most of these are administrative/reporting tools, not structured databases you can directly
query via API. The Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API) provides quality standards for Ayurvedic drugs but isn't a downloadable interaction database.
Here's why integrating IMPPAT + PubChem is brilliant for your project:
The interaction detection logic works like this:
: Get the phytochemicals in an Indian herb (e.g., Guggul contains
: Get the pharmacological profile of an allopathic drug (e.g., Warfarin inhibits Vitamin K)
— if both affect the same pathway (e.g., both thin blood via platelet aggregation), flag it as a risk
This is exactly how a 2025 paper by Apurva & Kumar proposed doing Ayurveda-based drug discovery
, and PubChem data with AI screening.
— with PubChem's drug data to build an AI-powered interaction detection engine," that sounds like
, not a hackathon toy. That's a huge differentiator.
You won't have time to build a full automated pipeline that queries both APIs in real-time and runs AI inference. Here's the realistic approach:
data for your top 30-40 Indian herbs from IMPPAT (their key phytochemicals and known mechanisms)
data for 20-30 common allopathic drugs from PubChem (their CYP450 interactions, mechanisms)
Build a curated interaction knowledge base
, show the architecture diagram with IMPPAT + PubChem integration and say "our prototype uses a curated subset; the full system would query these databases in real-time"
This is honest, technically sound, and impressive. You're showing real data infrastructure thinking, not just a toy demo.
This is a strong technical foundation
— the combination of IMPPAT + PubChem for herb-drug safety hasn't been done as a consumer tool, which is exactly your gap.
, and you’re right to think about it. In competitions, you
must not look like you ignored the problem statement
You don’t change your idea — you reframe it to FIT their problem.
That will get you disqualified or marked down.
anchor your solution inside their problem statement
“Our AI model predicts early disease risks related to diabetes, hypertension, and
— specifically those caused by unsafe drug–herb interactions.”
How to defend if judges question you
Judge: “This is not exactly disease prediction?”
early-stage health risks that directly lead to these diseases or their complications
These are early indicators and preventable triggers of serious conditions.”
“Most teams predict diseases from static inputs like age or lifestyle.
caused by drug–herb interactions — which are often more immediate and dangerous than the diseases themselves.”
Add ONE small feature to fully satisfy them:
If hypertensive → flag BP drop risks
Now you are 100% inside their problem statement.
If rules are strict, use this title:
“AI Model for Early Prediction of Disease Risks (Diabetes, Hypertension,
You are NOT outside the problem.
Solving a deeper layer of the same problem
Your idea is more unique than typical “predict diabetes from BMI” projects.
